Author: Srgg
Title: The Death of Coding Is Cancelled: Why Your AI Assistant Is Quickly Becoming an Imbecile
Text:
The Death of Coding Is Cancelled: Why Your AI Assistant Is Quickly Becoming an Imbecile
At the start of the project, it seems like a Demiurge. But that ends quickly and forever. Here’s a mathematical proof of why AI will never replace programmers.
I’ve got two pieces of news for you: one good, one bad.
The bad news: Building AGI on top of LLM is impossible.
The good news: if you’re an expert in a high-cognitive field, you’re probably safe from getting fired. Unless Sam Altman, Dario Amodei, and Demis Hassabis have already gotten inside your boss’s head with tall tales about AI replacing every worker.
Note: In addition to being an AI researcher, I’m also a professional web developer with years of experience at an outsourcing company. This post draws on personal experience in that field.
I keep hearing the same old refrain: the current generation of models works wonders. You ask it to build some app, hit “Enter,” and go grab a coffee. When you come back, everything is done and working.
As always — a gross exaggeration. And sometimes — outright fiction.
I’ll go further: for AI built on GPT, that outcome is unachievable — assuming we’re talking about a real product and not some toy demo with bells and whistles.
I’ve explained why many times before, but let me just share my observations instead.
Of course, I use AI in web development. Here’s what I’ve tried: Copilot, Cursor, Gemini Code Assist, Codex and Kilo. I also tried Antigravity from Google, but turned out to be too “smart”. In short, it was utterly incapable of holding a conversation with me like a normal human being.
OK, let’s be straight.
Sure, if AI only ever produced hallucinations, no one would use it. But no — in many cases it’s extremely useful. I’m not going to deny that. Moreover, I fully appreciate the complexity of the problems the LLM developers have managed to solve. I take my hat off to their engineering genius.
But.
It’s not enough. We’re not here for entertainment, are we? We need systems that are effective, reliable, and predictable.
And this is exactly where LLM and Generative AI have fundamentally unsolvable problems.
The most reliable tool for me so far has been Gemini Code Assist. When I first started using it, I was blown away. In the early hours of a new project it feels like magic: it works brilliantly. It writes code fast and explains everything. Its explanations are logical and coherent. You get the uncanny feeling that you’re not talking to an algorithm but to a highly skilled colleague-programmer.
So the era of “human coding” is over, and AGI (Artificial General Intelligence) is just one step away?
Not even close. And you start to realize this pretty quickly.
The moment your project grows beyond a toy example, the “magic” fades. You ask the AI a simple question, and… it “thinks.” At first it’s bearable. Sometimes five seconds, sometimes ten. But as the project grows, the thinking time balloons. It can sit there for a minute, two, five. Eventually you really can go grab that coffee while it hunts for an answer that a human expert would nail in seconds.
If you think this is a “bug” or a “slow server” — you’re wrong. This is a fundamental limit of the technology, and one piece of evidence that AGI built on LLM — is nothing more than a marketing fairy tale.
Math vs. Hype
So why does AI grind to such a halt? The answer lies in the transformer architecture itself. It’s called the Attention Mechanism.
At the start of a project, the context is clean. But the longer you work, the more the request “bloats.” LLM doesn’t “remember” your code the way a human does; on every request it re-reads all of the following from scratch:
- Chat history.
- Files.
- System instructions.
- Project indexes.
All of this turns into a colossal data array. And this is where physics enters the picture: computational complexity grows quadratically (O(n²)) with context length. Double the context — and the GPU load quadruples. Increase it tenfold — and the system starts devouring resources like a black hole.
Your AI assistant isn’t “thinking” about your code. It’s simply trying to digest terabytes of math operations to predict the next word. It operates like someone trapped in a closed cognitive loop.
Why a Programmer’s Brain Is Not a “Statistical Calculator”
This is the boundary that AI evangelists refuse to acknowledge. There is a fundamental difference between a human and an LLM system.
- A programmer doesn’t keep 10,000 lines of text in their head. They keep meaning. They understand the architecture, the intent, and the reasoning behind every decision. For AI, your project is just a flat sequence of symbols.
- The human brain excels at filtering out the irrelevant. But AI must process every single token it’s been “paid” with context to handle.
- A human learns on the job, whereas an LLM is static. It doesn’t get “smarter” from spending three hours helping you with refactoring. It just burns more energy.
The “Horse and Car” Trap
The LLM true believers will tell you: “The models will get better! Just wait for GPT-6 or Gemini XYZ”
This is a classic fallacy. You can breed a faster, hardier horse indefinitely. But you will never breed a car out of a horse.
Scaling LLMs is the path of quantitative improvement of a statistical predictor. But intelligence (AGI) requires:
- Causal reasoning.
- Autonomous goal-setting.
- Long-term memory that doesn’t burn down a power plant on every query.
The Uncomfortable Truth
At first, AI makes you faster. But the moment the system’s complexity ramps up, a human’s work speed starts to outpace the AI that has ground itself to a halt. You spend more time crafting prompts and waiting for inference than actually writing code.
I hope you now understand the source of my concern. We are building an economy on top of tools that physically cannot scale with the complexity of our problems. Today’s AI assistants are powerful statistical mirrors reflecting our own intelligence back at us. But don’t mistake the reflection for the thing itself.
Don’t believe Sam Altman when he says that by 2028 we’ll have an early version of AGI.
Although… if you really want to — go ahead and believe it.
But it won’t be by 2028, and any AGI that does emerge will be built on a completely different architecture. Which one exactly? I think you’ll find out soon enough.
